finding.vuln_claim: User-controlled `direction` is concatenated into the SQL string without allowlisting, unlike `sort`, enabling SQL injection in the `ORDER BY` clause.
The `url` query parameter is forwarded to `axios.get` without a working allowlist, enabling outbound requests to attacker-chosen hosts.
This state-changing PATCH handler updates persisted data with no shown CSRF token, Origin check, or double-submit cookie, so cookie-authenticated requests may be forged cross-site.
Replacing only the literal substring '../' once does not neutralize chained parent-directory segments before path.join resolves them.
Untrusted cookie bytes are unpickled, enabling arbitrary code execution during deserialization.
The access-control expression is malformed and cannot correctly enforce who may PATCH a given user id.
Unrestricted merge of `req.body` into the loaded user object allows mass assignment of sensitive attributes.
User-controlled markdown is rendered to HTML and stored without sanitization, enabling stored XSS when that HTML is later rendered in a browser.
A negative amount skips the insufficient-funds branch and flips the arithmetic so the sender gains funds while the recipient is debited.
Balance is read and checked without locking or atomic debit/credit, so concurrent requests can overdraw the same balance.
Recipient id comes from the client with no shown ownership or existence checks, enabling transfers to arbitrary or unintended accounts.
In production, failed signature verification falls back to jwt.decode, which returns an unauthenticated payload.
Authenticated callers can supply arbitrary user ids without a same-user or role check, enabling IDOR disclosure of other users' emails.
MAC verification uses `==`, which is not constant-time and can leak the expected digest via timing.
Path-only validation with `startswith('/')` still allows protocol-relative targets like `//evil.com`.
User-controlled XML is parsed with external entity resolution and network fetching enabled, enabling classic XXE.
User-controlled `req.body.email` and `req.body.password` are passed straight into `db.users.findOne(q)` as query keys/values without neutralizing NoSQL operators.
User-controlled `url` is passed to `requests.get`, causing SSRF because `startswith('http')` does not restrict hosts, schemes beyond the prefix, redirects, or internal addresses.
`requests.get(...).content` buffers the entire response body in memory without an explicit byte limit, enabling denial-of-service via large downloads.
gets() reads unbounded stdin into a fixed 64-byte stack buffer with no length check.
The bearer access token is written to SharedPreferences as cleartext XML, not hardware-backed encrypted storage.
A symmetric AES key is embedded as a hard-coded constant in source.
AES-CBC output is produced without an authentication tag or MAC over the ciphertext.
The condition on line 3 is inverted (`out` instead of `!out`), so a NULL `out` with `out_sz > 0` bypasses the early return and reaches `memcpy`.
The user-controlled `dir` value is concatenated into SQL without validation while only `sort` is allowlisted.
User-controlled `url` is passed to `axios.get` and echoed, enabling server-side requests to arbitrary hosts.
`request.json` can be `None`, so calling `.get` raises `AttributeError` and aborts the handler.
If `db.get_invoice` returns `None` for an unknown `inv_id`, accessing `inv.project_id` dereferences `None`.
path.join discards the /srv/data prefix when the joined segment is an absolute path, so the base directory is not enforced.
A single literal replace('../','') only strips the first occurrence and can be bypassed with residual '../' sequences or obfuscated dot-slash forms.
The `state` cookie is unpickled, letting attackers run code during deserialization.
Object.assign copies attacker-controlled req.body properties onto the loaded user object before persistence without field allowlisting.
The authorization guard expression is malformed (missing operators between req.user.id and req.user.isSupport), so it cannot correctly enforce intended access rules.
User body is passed through markdown.markdown and stored as HTML without sanitization, allowing stored XSS.
The signed integer amount is never constrained to be non-negative, so debits become credits and the payee is debited instead.
The balance check and the two balance writes are not performed as one atomic transaction, enabling a concurrent double-spend race.
Failed RS256 verification in production returns jwt.decode results, which are not cryptographically authenticated.
The handler uses client-supplied uid to fetch and return a user row without verifying uid matches the authenticated principal.
The HMAC tag is checked with `==`, which is not constant-time and can leak comparison progress via timing.
The MAC covers only the raw body, with no freshness token, so any captured valid `(body, X-Sig)` can be replayed.
Path-only allowlist via startswith('/') misses protocol-relative targets that still begin with '/'.
Untrusted request XML is parsed with external entities enabled and network fetches allowed, enabling XXE.
Unsanitized `req.body` fields are embedded in the `findOne` query object, allowing MongoDB/NoSQL operator injection.
Arbitrary http/https URLs from the client are fetched server-side with only a weak prefix check, enabling SSRF to internal or metadata endpoints.
gets() reads stdin with no length limit into a 64-byte stack buffer, enabling overflow.
The access token is written to SharedPreferences, which persists it as cleartext XML under the app’s private storage.
A fixed AES key is embedded in source, so anyone with the code or binary can decrypt all ciphertexts produced by enc().
AES-CBC output lacks an integrity tag, so ciphertext is malleable unless the application adds a separate MAC or uses an AEAD.
The guard `if (out || out_sz == 0)` is inverted, so non-NULL outputs are rejected while NULL destinations can reach `memcpy`.
User-controlled `direction` from `request.args` is interpolated into SQL while only `sort` is allowlisted, enabling SQL injection in the ORDER BY clause.

The `/fetch` handler forwards `req.query.url` to `axios.get` while the supposed URL restriction on line 3 is nullified because `//` starts a JavaScript line comment, leaving no working allowlist/bypass-safe validation.
Input validation for outbound fetches is malformed/bypassed, so untrusted URLs are accepted as a fetch target.
If get_invoice returns None for unknown ids, dereferencing inv.project_id before a 404 can yield errors or responses that differ from the forbidden path.
Returning the full inv.to_json() after updating only note may include unrelated sensitive invoice fields in the response.
`String.replace('../','')` removes only the first `../`, so values like `../../` still traverse above `/srv/data`.
If `f` is an absolute path, `path.join('/srv/data', f)` discards the base and reads `f` directly.
Untrusted cookie bytes are deserialized with pickle.loads, which can execute arbitrary code.
Saving after `Object.assign(user, req.body)` lets clients overwrite arbitrary user object properties stored in the database.
The `if` condition is syntactically invalid (`req.user.id req.user.isSupport`), so proper authorization between actor, target id, and support role cannot be enforced as written.
Untrusted comment bodies are passed through markdown and stored as HTML without sanitization, enabling stored cross-site scripting.
The transfer amount is cast to int but never constrained to be positive, so negative values invert debit/credit semantics.
Balance is read and checked separately from two writes without locking or atomic compare-and-swap, creating a classic check-then-act race.
In production, failed jwt.verify falls back to jwt.decode, accepting unverified tokens.
Any logged-in caller can supply an arbitrary user id and retrieve that user’s id and email because access is not restricted to the authenticated subject.
HMAC verification uses non-constant-time string equality, enabling timing side-channel analysis of the signature.
The handler reads and parses the full request body without an explicit size cap, enabling large-payload CPU/DoS via `json.loads`.
The `startswith('/')` gate allows protocol-relative targets like `//evil.com`, producing an off-site redirect.
XMLParser enables external entity resolution with network access, so attacker-controlled XML can trigger XXE.
With no_network=False, external entity resolution can force the parser to fetch attacker-chosen URLs server-side.
Login builds a database query object directly from request body fields passed to findOne, allowing operator injection.
User-controlled URL is passed to requests.get after only a trivial startswith('http') check, enabling SSRF to internal and external HTTP(S) targets.
gets() reads unbounded stdin into a 64-byte stack buffer with no length check.
The access token is persisted in unencrypted SharedPreferences instead of hardware-backed or encrypted storage.
A symmetric AES key is embedded as a constant in source code.
AES-CBC encryption is used without an integrity mechanism such as MAC or AEAD.
The guard uses `if (out || out_sz == 0)` instead of `if (!out || out_sz == 0)`, so `out == NULL` bypasses the error return and reaches `memcpy`.
The `dir` query parameter is concatenated into the SQL `ORDER BY` clause without allowlisting, enabling SQL injection while `sort` is whitelisted.
The handler forwards a user-controlled URL to axios.get without a working allowlist, causing server-side request forgery.

State-changing PATCH uses session-style current_user without any visible CSRF token or double-submit defense, enabling classic cookie-session CSRF if browsers send session cookies on cross-site requests.
Using request.json.get('note', inv.note) allows JSON null to overwrite the note because the key is present and the default is skipped.
Path traversal because only the first '../' is stripped so remaining '..' segments still escape the intended base via path.join normalization.
The `state` cookie is base64-decoded and passed to `pickle.loads`, deserializing attacker-controlled data.
The guard condition is malformed (missing boolean/relational operator), so intended owner-or-support checks likely never compile or never enforce correctly.
`Object.assign(user, req.body)` performs unsafe mass assignment of attacker-controlled fields onto the persisted user object.
User lookup uses `req.params.id` without a correct same-user authorization tie-in in the shown guard, enabling IDOR-style updates if access control is bypassed or misimplemented.
Untrusted comment text is passed through Markdown into HTML persisted for later rendering, which typically preserves raw HTML and yields stored XSS.
The insufficient-funds guard compares src to amt without rejecting non-positive amounts, so negative amt bypasses the check and inverts the balance updates.
Read-check-write on balances is not wrapped in a single atomic transaction or row lock, enabling concurrent requests to overdraw the same account.
When jwt.verify fails in production, the handler returns jwt.decode(t), which accepts arbitrary payloads without signature verification.
The route lets any logged-in caller supply an arbitrary user id with no binding to the session user, so object-level authorization is missing before returning id and email.
The HMAC tag is verified with ordinary string equality instead of a constant-time comparison, creating a timing side channel on the signature bytes.
`json.loads(body)` is not wrapped in error handling, so malformed JSON can raise and terminate the request handler unexpectedly.
The `startswith('/')` guard still allows protocol-relative URLs like `//evil.com`, which bypass same-origin intent and redirect off-site.
XXE risk: XMLParser resolves entities with no_network=False, so attacker XML can trigger external/public entity resolution.
DoS risk: entity resolution enables recursive/parameter entity expansion attacks (e.g., billion laughs) against the parser.
User-controlled `req.body` fields are spread into a MongoDB-style `findOne` query object, enabling NoSQL operator injection.
User-controlled URL is fetched server-side after only a trivial http prefix check, enabling SSRF to internal hosts and metadata endpoints.
Full HTTP response is read into memory via .content with no explicit size limit, enabling large-response memory pressure.
gets reads unlimited stdin into buf[64] with no bounds check, enabling a stack buffer overflow.
The access token is written to SharedPreferences without encryption or hardware-backed protection despite MODE_PRIVATE sandboxing.
AES key is hard-coded in source as a static 16-byte literal.
AES-CBC encryption returns ciphertext with no integrity or authenticity protection.
The condition `if (out || out_sz == 0)` is inverted so a NULL `out` with positive `out_sz` bypasses the guard and reaches `memcpy`.
